sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE].
My name is Lili, maiden name Jacob, last name Meier, M-E-I-E-R.
I was born in Bilky.
It's in the Carpathian Mountains.
It used to be Czechoslovakia after World War I.
And I had a-- we had a very good childhood.
My father was the provider.
And everything was going well.
I was born on January 16, 1926.
Today I'm 70.
I was-- and January is my birthday.
Martin.
Esther.
My father was a businessman.
A sister, I never had.
But I had five wonderful brothers.
Ari, Zisl, Hershey, Zrilu, and Selig.
It was a happy home.
Shabbat was like-- Thursday night, my mother started with the baking.
And then the [? challah ?] and the Danish went in on Friday in the oven.
It was a very happy childhood.
Oh yeah.
Well, the Shabbat was the usual chicken and chicken soup and matzo ball and-- like the baking, she did all herself.
It was just a joyous home and joyous parents.
Yes, they lived in the same town.
We mostly went to them, because they all wanted to have us there.
Yiddish.
In Bilky.
All of us, we were happy there.
This is where our parents, our grandparents, used to live and--
No, on the contrary.
The majority of my friends weren't Jewish.
But we got along, the Jews and the non-Jews.
We got along very well.
We had wonderful neighbors, and we would do for one another.
And it was always reciprocated.
It was a nice Jewish community.
I would say about 5,000 people lived there.
I was more of a thinker and a doer.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, it's not [? think. ?] You know, everything was just wonderful.
Yes.
We had friends whose-- whose family moved to America.
And then they would come and visit their-- their parents.
And that's one thing.
When I saw those people, you know, are coming to-- to Bilky, and the way they used to be dressed, and I said to myself, one day, I want to go to America.
Well, there was a club on Saturday.
They would gather.
But I was always just the bystander, because I was everywhere-- weddings, Jewish weddings, Gentile.
I was always a very curious child.
Yes.
My mother was a reader, just like my Eric is.
Well, usually my mother would borrow a book from the library.
And then she would say, sweetheart, I think you would like this book.
And it was about Tolstoy and those history books.
Oh, yeah.
Not the two youngest were, because.
Well, we were going on a Saturday to the synagogue.
And then family from out of town were arrive.
And it was a very festive time for us.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] on the last [INAUDIBLE] Passover, '44, somehow it was known in the town-- neighboring towns that they going to take the Jews and resettle them in Poland.
And it was like a-- a-- you know, story.
But soon it changed.
I decided in 1943, because they took up a-- my father's permit.
And I decided that, because a lot of youngsters were-- went to Budapest to work-- and that's what I went, too.
And I would send the money home.
We heard about Poland.
But from Germany, it was later on, a while later that we found out that what's going to go on.
We had a radio in the house which were-- we were not allowed to.
And we heard how he was [INAUDIBLE].
And they were talking about Hitler, and carrying on, and saying that he recruits the rich Jews, and the wives have to come and scrub the-- the [INAUDIBLE].
How-- how would you say that where the soldiers stayed?
So the-- those [? stop, ?] you know, German Jewish people were right away known as what they gonna-- you know, what's going to happen to them.
It was very rough, very rough.
But when he heard after dinner on that Passover night in '43, first, he says, no.
I have six-- I have six children.
And if I can feed five, I can feed a sixth one.
No child of mine will go to Budapest.
And then after dinner, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we sat down, and we talked.
[SIGHS] And he says, yeah, I know you are very strong-willed child.
But we let you do, because we trust you.
You never were a mischief.
You always know what you were doing.
And he's the one who gave in.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I went to Budapest.
I mean, we got on the train.
Friends from across the street of ours.
They were friends.
Esther Bohm.
She's in Israel.
I didn't have much to take with me.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I got on the train.
And that train would stop in every other town or city.
And there--
It was rough.
Well, what happened was-- can I go back to the train?
It was after Passover.
And the train was full of people who were going to Budapest.
And there was a lady sitting.
She must have been in her early 50s.
And she would-- she asked me do I speak Hungarian?
And I said, yes.
And where are you go?
Where are you going?
I says, I'm going to Budapest.
I want to help out my parents.
And she said, well, I-- and she introduced herself.
She says, I'm in charge of a big orphanage.
Would you like to come and work for us?
I says, no.
I says, these days are very bitter.
It's a very bitter pill to swallow.
And I says, and they'll put me in a more depressed mood.
And I said, I-- I was always taught by my parents that you should always feed an orphan the best way you can.
And she says, well, we are quite a while back from Budapest.
Why don't you think about it?
And-- and I says, well, I-- I decided not to really go.
She says, well, as we are [? progressing ?] on the train, we can talk.
She says, well, you can-- you can come and work for us.
And I was all of 18.
And all you have to do is make the list for the grocery, and you'll go to the store.
And you'll order everything, and they'll deliver it.
And I says, well, let me think about it.
But instead to going to the orphanage, I went to the Jewish community, because they were finding jobs for us.
And when I came there-- and there was a man, he says, what can we do for you?
And I told him I would like to get a permit and work.
Oh.
He-- he had a list there in front of him.
He says, oh, you don't have to worry.
You have a job.
And I says, where do I have a job?
He says-- and he told me the name of the orphanage, because the lady told me to.
And he says, we cannot give you, because she wants you.
And you'll-- you'll fit in just fine.
And I went along.

But as the days passed, and I missed my parents-- I was never away from them so far-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And by the time I came to the orphanage, I was knocked out, because I didn't sleep for days and nights.
And I found a friend there that I didn't see for about four years.
We were friends at-- at home.
Yeah.
Raisy.
In Hungarian, yeah.
R-O-N-A or R-O-N-O.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Street, 52nd Street.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Yes.
And we were very, you know, shocked to meet one another after so many years.
And she sounded so glad that we are together.
We grew up together, and now we're still friends.
And I said I'm very happy to-- to have found you, too.
At least I'll feel close that I have somebody that I know.
And I liked my job.
And I decided to stay there.
There were two children.
Oh-- Oh, let me go back.
Can I?
Sure.
That was actually not a real orphanage.
That was when they start taking the Slovakian Jews and the-- and the Czech Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] And the people would run away with the children, the parents.
And this orphanage was known to take care only of people who left their houses, their homes.
And there were active kids.
We-- we-- there was a little-- there was a [INAUDIBLE] that orphanage.
And the kids didn't go to school, because we had teachers there.
And they really took care of those children.
There was an incident once that I witnessed that a man came over, and he took one of the little girls on his lap.
And he kissed this kid.
She was about four years old.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the teacher came over.
And she says, can-- can we take Martha away, because she has to go to sleep?
She's very tired.
And then a couple of days later, I noticed that this-- the teacher was drilling this kid.
Martha, who was that man?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She started crying, and she shook her [? hands. ?] She says, don't talk to me.
I'm very sad.
Finally, they calmed her down, and she stopped crying.
But she also had a brother there.
There were two of them-- Robi, and her name was Martha.
And there were about, I would say, about 100 kids, all for the same reason, that orphanage.
And [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] she said, Martha, who-- who was that man?
Tell me.
I won't tell anybody.
And she started crying and crying.
All of the sudden, she said, that's my mother's husband.
And we-- the teachers trained those children not to reveal, because we had to-- and they were coming, the officials, to check what kind of an orphanage.
And we were like, say, about 500 feet from a munition factory there.
And those sirens were terrible.
The kids were scared.
We also-- We also had [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] downstairs-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
It was, when the kids, you know, couldn't go out and play, so they would play down in that-- I forgot how you call.
And those kids somehow, I'll never forget, and I never did forget.
Once I saw a lady looking through the fence where the kids were playing on, you know, on a sunny day.
And this lady somehow passed out.
She was on the other side of the fence.
And the kids were very sad, because she didn't come to look for them, look at them.
And I just never forgot those kids.
Can I skip a little bit?
Well, I'd like to try to keep it in the sequence.
So how long did you remain in the orphanage?
13 months.
Yes, I did.
A neighbor-- we didn't have a telephone there, but a neighbor told me that I could use the phone any time I want to call my parents.
I was very lonely.
I missed them.
Yes.
I went a few times.
And then one day, we heard what's coming up.
Well, we heard that they take away-- they took [INAUDIBLE].
They took so far away the-- the Jews from Slovakia, from the Czech countries.
And our country-- where I was born, Bilky, became Hungary.
And in order for us to be able to survive from Bilky, we needed to prove that, you know, we are Jews, and we do live in Hungary, which wasn't true.
But that's-- that's how they, you know, let it go.
Next, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call those people that-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I was-- I went to order the-- in the grocery store, what we needed.
And when I came back, a friend of mine came there, and she said, so you're gonna stay in Budapest?
I said, no.
Well, I'm gonna stay, because they're gonna take us away to Poland somewhere.
And I said, aren't you coming home to your parents?
She said, no.
I want to stay here.
That's OK.
Well, on the way to the grocery store, I made a U-turn.
And I-- [? when ?] I heard a rally there.
And the rally was in German.
And I didn't understand German at that time.
So there were so many people, and he was screaming and banging there on the table in Germany.
And one lady says in Hungarian, well, it looks like this man came for all the Jews, to take all the Jews away.
And I just walked away.
I didn't want to say nothing.
What if they arrest me or something?
I just walked away.
And I ended up going to the train.
I had enough money on me to go-- to go home.
And my-- this friend of mine hollered after me, you're crazy.
Why don't you come-- stay here?
What if something happens?
And I said, if something is gonna happen, I want to be there, too.
Otherwise, I would never forgive myself.
And I ended up going home.
We were home for two weeks.
And then one night, on a Thursday night-- it was after Passover.
And [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] my mother and I decided to prepare the-- the baking for tomorrow morning and then the cooking.
And all of a sudden, it was like 12:00, 1:00-- 1:00 in the morning, there was a knock on-- on-- on our door.
We never had the doors locked.
It wasn't needed to lock doors there.
And I asked my mother, should I open the door?
She says, yes.
You can open the door.
And a neighbor came in.
And he says he just came from a meeting that they're going to uproot us, and we-- we are going to Poland.
Was this neighbor Jewish?
Pardon me?
Yes, he was Jewish.
Isaac something.
I don't remember his last name.
Why don't you go have your coffee?
